Coterra's quarterly dividend is $0.22 per share now, adding up to around $671 million per year with 763 million outstanding shares.

It paid off $250 million in term loans in Q1 2025 and intends on paying the remaining $750 million back during 2025. The remainder of its free cash flow may go towards share repurchases, which are expected to accelerate in 2H 2025.

Notes On Valuation

I've revised Coterra's estimated value downwards from approximately $32 per share to $28 per share now. Coterra's early 2025 operational performance was solid, but I've reduced my long-term (after 2026) commodity prices to $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. My long-term natural gas price is unchanged from the last time I looked at Coterra, but I've lowered my long-term oil price by $5.

Natural gas strip prices for 2026 are pretty strong right now, at around $4.35. This mostly offsets the impact of high-$50s oil on Coterra's free cash flow, so Coterra's 2026 free cash flow may end up being slightly higher than its 2025 free cash flow.

The general shift in capex towards natural gas development (from oil development) leads me to currently expect more of a $70 oil and $3.75 natural gas environment beyond 2026, though.

Conclusion

Coterra modified its 2025 development plans to shift capex from the Permian to its Marcellus assets, while reducing its overall budget by $100 million. This makes sense given the relatively strong natural gas prices and weaker oil prices.

At the current strip, Coterra is projected to generate $2.07 billion in free cash flow in 2025 now. Coterra's early production results were a bit above expectations, and it has maintained its full-year oil production guidance and slightly increased its total production guidance despite some issues with its Harkey wells.

Coterra has been generally performing fine operationally. However, due to lower long-term oil pricing expectations, I have reduced my estimate of Coterra's value from around $32 per share to $28 per share.

data.title: uniQure: A Buy For Their Lead In Huntington's Program, In Light Of PTC's Data
data.description:
uniQure (QURE) is an Amsterdam-based, gene therapy developer that has a proud history of multiple "First" accomplishments (see below).

(Source)

Currently, the company has 1 approved gene therapy, 4 in clinical stage, and 2 in preclinical stage (see below).

As seen above, AMT-130 for Huntington's Disease [HD] is their most advanced clinical-stage asset, which is said to be on an accelerated approval pathway.

HD is a serious genetic disease that currently has no cure or disease-modifying treatment.

By this market report, the HD treatment space is estimated to reach $1.4B by 2034, from the present $717M in 2025.

In Nov 2024, Novartis (NVS) is reported to have paid $1B upfront, plus $1.9B in [future] bio-bucks for PTCT's PTC518, a small molecule HD drug, a phase 2 asset.

In my opinion, both these news suggest that the prospect of QURE's lead program, AMT-130 for HD, will be consequential, both for the patients and all the stakeholders, e.g. the company and the shareholders.

Next, let's look at AMT-130's status and the disclosed data so far.

QURE's HD gene therapy: Status & Data

1. Status

In Nov 2024, QURE met with the FDA to discuss their HD program via an accelerated approval pathway, as summarized in their Jan 2025 presentation.

(Green box added by Author)

While all five are positive, I highlight the item which, I think, is most significant from the meeting, which indicates that "an additional pre-submission study", e.g. a registrational phase 3, "is not required for the Accelerated Approval pathway."

I will discuss this point further below, but for now, suffice to note that in the Nov 2024 meeting, the FDA was aligned with the idea that QURE does not need to conduct another HD trial before they can file via the accelerated approval pathway.

Given the FDA's guidance, QURE is currently working on their BLA [Biologics License Applications] submission (see slide 14) and their expected near-term milestones (see slide 15).

2. Data

According to QURE, there are two results that would "form the base" of QURE's BLA, namely cUHDRS (composite Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale) data, relative to "external control" and "reductions in CSF NfL" results.

I will further discuss cUHDRS results shortly, in relation to PTCT's data, but for now, suffice to note that for QURE, both results are positive.

Let's now turn to the results of an HD phase 2 trial from another HD drug developer.

PTCT's HD phase 2 data

On Monday, May 5, PTC Therapeutics (PTCT) announced results from their phase 2 HD trial for their drug, PTC518.

According to the company,

The study met its primary endpoint of reduction in blood Huntingtin [HTT] protein levels (p<0.0001) at Week 12 and favorable safety and tolerability. In addition, the 12-month data from the Stage 2 patients are consistent with the previously reported dose-dependent lowering of HTT protein and dose-dependent trends across clinical scales.

To this apparently positive data, the market reaction seemed contrary, e.g. an 18% drop (see below).

For anyone who is new to HD drug trials, 'lowering blood HTT protein levels' is a surrogate [efficacy] endpoints, which means that while promising, it remains uncertain that successful surrogate-endpoint data would translate to real efficacy [in slowing or modifying disease progression].

For HD, the commonly accepted, real efficacy endpoint is called cUHDRS, which assesses the changes in HD patients' functional capacity, motor function, cognitive abilities.

In a presentation for Monday's release, PTCT included the 12-month results of cUHDRS from their phase 2 trial with PTC518 (see below).

(color boxes added by author)

One important thing to know: for cUHDRS data, the lower scores indicate worsening disease.

In the above slide, the lowest [worst-disease-progression] number is highlighted by red boxes; while the highest number is highlighted by green boxes.

Few notables:

  • Dose-dependent benefit is seen in stage 2 patients only.
  • In stage 3 patients, PTC518 10mg (the higher dose of the two doses studied) performed worse than the placebo and the worsening, 0.61=(1.43-0.82) is greater than the benefit of PTC518 5mg vs. the placebo, 0.04=(0.82-0.78)

I will discuss more on this next, but for now, please note that for cUHDRS results, the 12-month results are mixed [only positive in stage 2 patients], and failed in stage 3 patients [placebo outperformed the 10mg dose].

Below is the 12-month results showing PTC's HTT protein-lowering data.

As stated in the PR, blood HTT lowering is this trial's primary endpoint, which, seen above, was met successfully (p<0.0001)

Namely, in both Stage 2 and Stage 3 HD patients, at Month 12, PTC518 treatment "Resulted in Dose-Dependent [higher dose worked better] Blood HTT Lowering", relative to the placebo.

According to this Endpoints News coverage:

PTC previously said that it met with the FDA in December [2024] to discuss huntingtin protein lowering as a potential surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval. The agency “was aligned with the scientific rationale” for the surrogate endpoint, but asked PTC to provide data to show associations between huntingtin protein lowering and changes in clinical outcome measures.

(bold & italic added for emphasis)

If such "associations" are required by the FDA for an accelerated approval pathway in HD, I'd estimate that PTC's 12-month data has made this prospect more uncertain.

Namely, the data shown so far indicates that statistically significant, dose-dependent HTT protein lowering 12 months data does NOT lead to the dose-dependent, statistically significant cUHDRS outcomes.

In my opinion, inconsistent cUHDRS data, vs. positive HTT protein lowering data, may explain Monday's market reaction.

While we're on the topic of cUHDRS data, let's turn to one more item.

In PTCT's presentation, they include 24-month interim data from stage 2 patients, when comparing to external control [not from their trial's placebo, but HD patients from natural history studies].

Similar to their 12-month Stage 2 patient results, the 24-month results showed a dose-dependent, 5mg (n=10) vs. 10mg (n=11), positive cUHDRS data, relative to the external control (see below).

(Color Boxes added by the Author).

As before, I highlighted the worst cUHDRS score, -1.1 in control, with a red box, and the best score, -0.80, with the green box.

As noted previously, QURE said that the FDA may allow cUHDRS data, relative to external control to serve as the clinical outcome. Here we've an opportunity to compare apples to apples for two HD drug candidates.

Below is QURE's 24-month data, relative to the external control.

(Source; color boxes added by author)

One should always note that any cross-trial comparison [comparing data from different trials] is speculative and should not be viewed as conclusive.

However, given that these two HD trials are similar in size (total of 21 patients for both) and use external controls which seem to share a similar 24-month cUHDRS score (PTCT's -1.1 vs. QURE's -1), the best performer's (higher dose in both cases) cUHDRS score could be illustrative of their respective [potential] efficacy (see table below).

PTCT's QURE's

24-month cUHDRS change from baseline

(larger the change, worsening disease)

 -0.8 -0.2

delta between treatment vs. external control

(larger the delta, greater the treatment benefit)

 0.3 0.8

(compiled by author; bold numbers for best data)

As remarked previously, any cross-trial comparison is speculative, and should not be viewed as conclusive evidence.

However, for our purpose here, it's important to note that at 24-month, in these two small (n=21) HD trials with external control, QURE's treatment seems to be more effective in slowing the disease progression, and with a greater benefit than PTCT's.

Discussion

In the HD treatment space, there is another developer, Wave Life Sciences (WVE), which has completed a phase 1/2 trial.

According to WVE's 2024 annual report, the company "expects to submit an Investigational New Drug (“IND”) application for a potentially registrational Phase 2/3 study of WVE-003 in HD in the second half of 2025".

From this, one can conclude that WVE's HD program is at least another trial, i.e. their phase 2/3 trial, away from being ready for the filing, if such a trial is successfully completed, which presumably will take 2-3 years minimum.

This would suggest that in the HD space, QURE is likely to be the first company with positive data, and FDA alignment to be ready to file an application via the accelerated approval pathway.

If successfully filed/accepted and if successful in obtaining the accelerated approval in due course, these catalysts would no doubt be very impactful in meeting a serious unmet medical needs, and for the company's prospect.

Both these are big "if"s and therein lies the investment thesis for this company.

If both turn out to be successful, the stock is likely to increase significantly, with the expectation of a sizable market; conversely, if one or both are unsuccessful, the stock will likely decrease significantly, due to the loss of a near-term market opportunity.

There is one more notable item that may be relevant here.

On Tuesday, May 6, R&D biotech stocks seemed to have a sector-wide sell-off, with a few names, e.g. SRPT, NTLA, QURE, CAPR etc., being hit with >20% crash.

In this BioSpace coverage, the reporter suggests that such a dramatic sector-wide sell-off was the biotech investors' response to the appointment of FDA's CBER, which regulates vaccines, cell/gene therapies, and blood supply.

For our purpose here, it's not beyond the realm of possibility that the CBER, under the new director, could depart radically or negate all together the agreements/alignments/standards that were established previously.

In my opinion, if that were to occur, it would be most unfortunate and damaging towards the endeavor of drug R&D.

Specific to QURE's HD program, that would likely mean that the Nov 2024 meeting may no longer be a valid/reliable guidance and that QURE's plan to submit their BLA, without conducting a new trial, via the accelerated approval pathway, could be totally upended.

Financials

According to QURE's 2024 annual report, as of Dec 31, 2024, the company has a cash runway of $160M, and ~$183M net cash used in operating activities in 2024 (see below).

Risks & Concluding Thoughts

Biotech investing has never been easy, especially for investors in the small-cap R&D companies.

QURE, as an investment opportunity, is no different. The stock is volatile and can be driven up or down by reasons that are company-specific or are beyond the company's control.

In addition to that, QURE's burn rate is high and therefore the financial risks of dilution is always present.

Any delay or failure in the regulatory progress, such as their lead HD program, and any delay or failure in trial data, HD or others, will all cause the stock to tumble with no certainty of a timely recovery.

All this is to say that for anyone interested here, please consider the risks involved, and the timeframe required for an investment to succeed.

In my opinion, only risk what you're ready to lose and in a timeframe that you're not likely to panic and sell, for reasons that are immaterial.

Thanks for reading. All the best!
